column1:
Fix this sentence:
A veterinarian are educated than landscaper.
column2:
✅ Veterinarians are better educated than landscapers.

📖 A comparison needs a comparative form plus 'than': say 'better educated', not just 'educated'. Also, to talk about a profession in general, use the bare plural (veterinarians / landscapers) and make the verb agree (are). 'A veterinarian are' mixes a singular subject with a plural verb.

e.g.
• Engineers are better paid than teachers.
• City doctors are better trained than rural ones.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Become more a veterinarian.
column2:
✅ become a veterinarian

📖 Drop the extra 'more' — it doesn't belong here. To say you enter a profession, the pattern is 'become a + job'. The teacher kept repeating just 'Become.' to signal that the extra words were unnecessary.

e.g.
• He wants to become a vet.
• She became a teacher after college.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
anyone went to the college can choose this major
column2:
✅ anyone who went to college can choose this major

📖 You need the relative pronoun 'who' to link the clause to 'anyone'; without it the sentence has two verbs and no connection between them. Also drop 'the': 'go to college' (like 'go to school') takes no article when it means attending as a student.

e.g.
• Anyone who finishes the course gets a certificate.
• Students who go to college can apply for this scholarship.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I need to focus my English skills and my family.
column2:
✅ I need to focus on my English skills and my family.

📖 'Focus' meaning 'concentrate on' always takes the preposition 'on': focus on something. Don't drop it.

e.g.
• Let's focus on the main problem.
• This year I want to focus on my health.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Only my daughter or my wife want to watch a movie, and then I will company with them.
column2:
✅ I will accompany them.

📖 'Company' is a noun; the verb meaning 'go somewhere with someone' is 'accompany'. It is transitive, so it takes a direct object with no 'with': 'accompany them', not 'company with them'.

e.g.
• I'll accompany you to the hospital.
• She accompanied her mother to the market.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
They can't earn money. They need to ask money for their parents.
column2:
✅ They need to ask their parents for money. / They need to ask them for it.

📖 The pattern is 'ask somebody for something': the person comes right after 'ask', then 'for + the thing'. 'Ask money for their parents' reverses the roles and means something else. (The teacher's 'ask it from their parents' also works.)

e.g.
• He asked his boss for a raise.
• Don't be afraid to ask me for help.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
He built his own company, so he recruit many students. They recruit some students to help.
column2:
✅ He recruited many students. / They recruited some students to help.

📖 When you narrate past events, keep the verbs in the past tense: recruit -> recruited. You dropped the past '-ed' several times in a row. Watch verb endings ('-ed' for past, '-s' for he/she/it) — it is a recurring pattern for you. See also [[2026-05-28-008]].

e.g.
• He recruited several interns last summer.
• They hired students for part-time work.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If someone become the soccer coach, they can earn a lot.
column2:
✅ If someone becomes a good soccer coach, they can earn a lot.

📖 (assistant note) After a third-person singular subject ('someone'), the present-tense verb needs '-s': becomes. Use 'a' (a soccer coach) since you are not pointing to one specific, already-known coach. Same verb-ending weakness as [[2026-05-28-007]].

e.g.
• If someone becomes a doctor, they can help many people.
• When she becomes a manager, she will travel a lot.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Ground water means the water, we can see it on the ground.
column2:
✅ Groundwater is the water that you see on the ground.

📖 Don't split a definition into two comma-spliced clauses ('the water, we can see it...'). Join them with a relative pronoun: 'the water that you see on the ground'. The teacher pointed out this is exactly what conjunctions and relative pronouns are for — keep a definition to one clean sentence.

e.g.
• A greenhouse is a place where you grow plants.
• An eyewitness is a person who sees an event happen.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Sorry teacher, what's the public speaker?
column2:
✅ Excuse me, could you explain what a public speaker is? / Sorry, what does "public speaker" mean?

📖 (assistant note) 'Sorry teacher' is a word-for-word translation from Chinese. To politely interrupt or ask, start with 'Excuse me' or 'Sorry,' and follow with a full question. Also, when asking what a type of thing is, use 'a' (general) rather than 'the' (a specific, already-known one): 'what's a public speaker'.

e.g.
• Excuse me, what does that word mean?
• Sorry, could you explain what a barista is?

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I think that's because there should are interesting and comedy.
column2:
✅ because they are interesting and humorous

📖 Use 'they are' (subject + be), not 'there should are', which is ungrammatical. And describe the shows with the adjective 'humorous', not the noun 'comedy'. So: 'because they are interesting and humorous.'

e.g.
• I watch them because they are funny and relaxing.
• The show is interesting and humorous.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
They can spend money for the soccer team.
column2:
✅ They can spend money on the soccer team.

📖 (assistant note) The collocation is 'spend money on something', not 'for'. Use 'spend time/money on + the thing or activity'.

e.g.
• Fans spend a lot of money on their idols.
• I don't want to spend money on things I don't need.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Reading compound nouns as separate words: takeaway -> 'take her away', popcorn -> 'pop a coin', courthouse -> 'got our house', textbook -> 'text the book', eyeball -> 'I ball'.
column2:
✅ takeaway / popcorn / courthouse / textbook / eyeball — one blended word, stressed on the first part.

📖 A compound noun is a single word: blend it together and don't insert extra syllables or articles in the middle. Most noun+noun compounds are stressed on the first element (TAKEaway, POPcorn, COURThouse, TEXTbook, EYEball). This was the focus of today's lesson.

e.g.
• POPcorn ['pɒpkɔːn], not pop-a-corn
• TEXTbook ['tekstbʊk], not text-the-book

column1:
Fix this sentence:
There are many greenhouse there.
column2:
✅ There are many greenhouses there.

📖 After 'many', a countable noun must be plural: many greenhouses. The teacher modeled the plural ('so many greenhouses there'). Same missing '-s' pattern as your verb endings in [[2026-05-28-007]] and [[2026-05-28-008]].

e.g.
• There are many greenhouses in my hometown.
• She visited many countries last year.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We need to collect many information from anywhere.
column2:
✅ We need to collect a lot of information from anywhere.

📖 (assistant note) 'Information' is uncountable: it has no plural and doesn't take 'many'. Use 'a lot of information' / 'much information', and never 'informations'.

e.g.
• We collected a lot of information for the report.
• There isn't much information about it online.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
When we meet someone we need to handshake with them.
column2:
✅ When we meet someone we shake hands with them.

📖 (assistant note) 'Handshake' is a noun (the gesture). The action is the verb phrase 'shake hands with someone'. So 'shake hands with them', not 'handshake with them'. Same noun-used-as-verb slip as [[2026-05-28-005]] (company/accompany).

e.g.
• It's polite to shake hands with your host.
• They shook hands and sat down.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Veterinarians are educated than landscapers.
column2:
✅ Veterinarians are better educated than landscapers.

📖 A comparison needs a comparative form. 'educated than' is incomplete; use 'better educated than' (comparative of the adverb 'well') or 'more educated than'.

e.g.
• She is better educated than her brother.
• This phone is more advanced than mine.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
There are too many traffics in it, so we want to reduce it.
column2:
✅ There is too much traffic, so we want to reduce it.

📖 'Traffic' is an uncountable (mass) noun: it has no plural 'traffics' and takes 'much' + a singular verb, not 'many' + 'are'. Say 'too much traffic'.

e.g.
• There is too much traffic during rush hour.
• How much traffic was there on the way?

column1:
Fix this sentence:
During my visited my hometown, there were too many people.
column2:
✅ During my visit to my hometown, there were too many people.

📖 After the possessive 'my' (and the preposition 'during') you need the noun 'visit', not the verb 'visited'. Use the pattern 'during my visit to + place'.

e.g.
• During my visit to Beijing, I saw the Great Wall.
• On my first visit to the city, I got lost.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
There are too many people in the highway.
column2:
✅ There are too many people on the highway.

📖 Use 'on' with roads, streets and highways, not 'in': 'on the highway / on the road'.

e.g.
• There was an accident on the highway.
• Don't play on the road.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
It was happened, a serious traffic event.
column2:
✅ There was a serious car accident.

📖 'Happen' is intransitive and never passive, so 'was happened' is wrong. To introduce an event, use 'There was + noun' or 'A serious accident happened.'

e.g.
• There was a serious car accident on the highway.
• A big earthquake happened last night.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Many people prepared this problem, so they bring a portable toilet.
column2:
✅ Many people are prepared for this problem, so they bring a portable toilet.

📖 'Prepare' needs the preposition 'for' before the thing you are getting ready against: 'be prepared for something'. 'prepared this problem' is missing 'for'.

e.g.
• You should be prepared for emergencies.
• We prepared for the exam all week.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We couldn't find any parking lot, so we went away.
column2:
✅ We couldn't find any parking lot, so we drove away.

📖 When you leave by car, 'drive away' is more precise and natural than the general 'go away'.

e.g.
• He got angry and drove away.
• We drove off without saying goodbye.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The dining table that I bought, I put on the network, was pretty expensive.
column2:
✅ The dining table that I bought online was very expensive.

📖 Keep the relative clause together: 'the dining table (that) I bought online'. Don't split the subject with an extra clause, and 'buy online' is the natural phrase, not 'put on the network'.

e.g.
• The shoes I bought online were too small.
• The car that he sold last week was very old.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you don't have an apartment in this area, you can't send your children to the high school.
column2:
✅ If you don't have an apartment in this area, you can't send your children to high school.

📖 Institutions used for their normal purpose take no article: 'go to / send to school, high school, university, hospital'. 'the high school' would mean one specific building.

e.g.
• My son goes to high school.
• She wants to go to university next year.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We can sit around the coffee table to have a rest.
column2:
✅ We can sit around the coffee table to rest.

📖 'Rest' is also a verb, so 'to rest' is shorter and more natural than 'to have a rest'. Use the verb directly after 'to'.

e.g.
• Let's stop and rest for a while.
• I need to rest after work.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Under the moonlight, it's too difficult find the way go home.
column2:
✅ Under the moonlight, it's too difficult to find our way back home.

📖 'too + adjective' is followed by 'to + verb': 'too difficult to find'. Also use the set phrase 'find our way back home' with the possessive 'our'.

e.g.
• It was too dark to see anything.
• We finally found our way back home.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We need to build a building to prevent earthquake and storm.
column2:
✅ We need to build a building with a stronger structure to prevent an earthquake or a storm from destroying the infrastructure.

📖 Singular countable nouns need an article: 'an earthquake', 'a storm'. Also use 'prevent something from doing something' to say what the damage would be.

e.g.
• A vaccine prevents people from getting sick.
• We must protect houses from being damaged by a storm.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
People like to eat sweet food because we find it a good way to help us improve well being.
column2:
✅ People like to eat sweet food because they find it a good way to improve our well-being.

📖 Don't drop possessive pronouns: it is 'improve our well-being', not 'improve well-being'. Keep the subject consistent too — 'people ... they', not 'people ... we'.

e.g.
• Exercise improves our well-being.
• People should take care of their own health.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
It's a sunny day today, so the weather was pretty good.
column2:
✅ It's a sunny day today, so the weather is pretty good.

📖 Use the present tense for a situation that is true now. Since it is sunny today, say 'the weather is pretty good', not 'was'.

e.g.
• It's raining now, so the roads are wet.
• Today is hot, so the weather is uncomfortable.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
There are fewer and fewer people becoming a bus driver.
column2:
✅ There are fewer and fewer people who become bus drivers.

📖 When the subject ('people') is plural, the job noun must be plural too: 'become bus drivers'. With 'fewer and fewer people', a relative clause 'who become...' reads more naturally.

e.g.
• Fewer young people want to become teachers.
• Many of them became doctors.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We have many automatic cars to instead them.
column2:
✅ We can use automatic cars to replace them.

📖 'Instead' is an adverb, not a verb, so 'to instead them' is wrong. The verb you want is 'replace' (replace someone/something). The phrase form is 'instead of'.

e.g.
• Machines are replacing workers.
• We used tea instead of coffee.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
A newspaper reporter need to face many serious problems.
column2:
✅ Newspaper reporters need to face a lot of problems.

📖 A singular subject 'a reporter' takes 'needs', not 'need'. For people in general the plural is more natural: 'reporters need'.

e.g.
• Teachers need a lot of patience.
• A teacher needs a lot of patience.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
More and more people gave up their career.
column2:
✅ More and more people give up their careers.

📖 For a general, ongoing trend use the present simple 'give up', not the past 'gave up'. Also each person has a career, so use the plural 'careers'.

e.g.
• More and more people work from home these days.
• Many young people give up their jobs to travel.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The man we met yesterday is a choir director.
column2:
✅ The man we met yesterday was a choir director.

📖 Because the time marker is 'yesterday', the main verb must be past: 'was a choir director'. Keep tenses consistent with the time you mention.

e.g.
• The woman I met yesterday was very kind.
• The film we watched last night was boring.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The choir director gave us a great performance, so we need to thanks for it.
column2:
✅ The choir director gave us a great performance, so we should be grateful for it.

📖 'Thanks' is informal and is not used as a verb this way. In a more formal sentence use 'be grateful for' or 'thank + someone'. '(be) appreciate' is also wrong — 'appreciate' takes a direct object: 'we appreciate it'.

e.g.
• We are grateful for your help.
• I really appreciate your support.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
One of my friends is the orchestra conductor. He always join our meeting and give us interesting music.
column2:
✅ One of my friends is an orchestra conductor. He always joins our meetings and gives us interesting performances.

📖 Third-person singular 'he' needs '-s': 'joins', 'gives'. Use 'an orchestra conductor' (one of several, not 'the'), and a conductor gives a 'performance', not 'music'.

e.g.
• He always helps his classmates.
• She gives us a lot of homework.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Today I had time to rest, so that's make me happy.
column2:
✅ Today I had time to rest, so that made me happy.

📖 Talking about a past event takes the past tense: 'made', not 'make'. Don't put 'be' before a bare verb ('that's make' is wrong); say 'that made me happy'.

e.g.
• The good news made me happy.
• Seeing my family made me feel better.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I recited some English words and listened some English podcasts.
column2:
✅ I recited some English words and listened to some English podcasts.

📖 'Listen' needs the preposition 'to' before its object: 'listen to something'.

e.g.
• I listen to music every morning.
• She listened to the teacher carefully.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I rarely eat heavy meal before sleep.
column2:
✅ I rarely eat heavy meals before bed.

📖 'Meal' is countable, so use the plural 'meals' when speaking generally. Also use the set phrase 'before bed' (or 'before I sleep'), not 'before sleep'.

e.g.
• I never eat heavy meals before bed.
• Don't drink coffee before bed.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I never dreamed about a ghost.
column2:
✅ I never dreamed of a ghost.

📖 When 'dream' means seeing something while asleep, use 'dream of': 'dream of a ghost'. ('dream about' is possible too, but the teacher wanted 'of' here.)

e.g.
• I dreamed of flying last night.
• She often dreams of her childhood home.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
You won't have enough energy to fix your daily work.
column2:
✅ You won't have enough energy to do your daily chores.

📖 We 'do' chores/work, not 'fix' them ('fix' means repair). For everyday tasks the natural noun is 'chores'.

e.g.
• I have to do my chores after dinner.
• He does the housework on weekends.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you can't sleep well, you will have some mental problems.
column2:
✅ If you can't sleep well, you will suffer from mental problems.

📖 For illnesses and conditions the natural verb is 'suffer from': 'suffer from mental problems', rather than 'have some mental problems'.

e.g.
• He suffers from anxiety.
• Many old people suffer from memory loss.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Your company will be disappoint.
column2:
✅ Your company will be disappointed.

📖 To describe how someone feels, use the -ed participle as an adjective: 'be disappointed'. 'disappoint' is the base verb and can't follow 'be' like this.

e.g.
• The manager was disappointed with the results.
• I'm disappointed in myself.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
We are a retail company, so we more focus on to sell good to customer.
column2:
✅ We are a retail company, so we are more focused on selling goods to customers.

📖 Use 'be focused on + -ing': 'we are focused on selling'. After the preposition 'on' use the gerund (selling), not 'to sell'. Also 'goods' and 'customers' should be plural.

e.g.
• We are focused on improving quality.
• The store sells goods to customers all over the country.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Twenty years ago, most people didn't get an education, so they believe in this kind of thing.
column2:
✅ Twenty years ago, most people didn't get an education, so they believed in this kind of thing.

📖 The sentence is about the past ('twenty years ago'), so the verb must be past too: 'believed', to match the rest of the sentence.

e.g.
• Long ago, people believed the earth was flat.
• As a child, I believed in Santa Claus.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
I'm not watched this movie more than ten times.
column2:
✅ I didn't watch this movie more than ten times.

📖 For a negative statement in the simple past, use 'didn't + base verb': 'I didn't watch'. 'I'm not watched' mixes up the structures.

e.g.
• I didn't watch TV last night.
• We didn't see that film.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Mr. Wright, whom we met yesterday, is a gracious person. He always help us finish our tasks.
column2:
✅ Mr. Wright, whom we met yesterday, is a gracious person. He always helps us finish our tasks.

📖 Third-person singular 'he' needs '-s' in the present simple: 'he helps'.

e.g.
• She always helps her classmates.
• He works very hard.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
He is gracious; he didn't ask money and did everything for free.
column2:
✅ He is gracious; he didn't ask for money and did everything for free.

📖 Use 'ask for' when requesting something: 'ask for money'. The full pattern is 'ask + someone + for + something' (ask us for money). 'ask money' is missing 'for'.

e.g.
• He asked for help.
• The man asked us for directions.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
He did those things for free, so I appreciate for him.
column2:
✅ He did those things for free, so I appreciated him.

📖 'Appreciate' is transitive and takes a direct object with no preposition: 'I appreciate it / I appreciated him'. Use the past 'appreciated' to match 'did'.

e.g.
• I really appreciate your help.
• We appreciated everything she did.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
He asked us for money, but we weren't familiar with him, so we run away.
column2:
✅ He asked us for money, but we weren't familiar with him, so we ran away.

📖 This is a past-tense story, so use the irregular past 'ran', not 'run'.

e.g.
• The thief ran away when he saw the police.
• We ran to catch the bus.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
It's just like learning language.
column2:
✅ It's just like learning a language.

📖 'Language' here is a countable noun, so it needs the article 'a': 'learning a language'.

e.g.
• Learning a language takes time.
• Playing an instrument is a useful skill.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
My friends always forget bring their kids to go out.
column2:
✅ My friends always forget to bring their kids outside.

📖 'Forget' is followed by the infinitive 'to + verb' when you mean you failed to do something: 'forget to bring'. Also use 'outside', not 'to go out', after 'bring their kids'.

e.g.
• I always forget to lock the door.
• She took her kids outside to play.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Eating a lot of fish improve memory.
column2:
✅ Eating a lot of fish improves memory.

📖 The subject is the gerund phrase 'eating a lot of fish', which is singular, so the verb takes '-s': 'improves'.

e.g.
• Reading every day improves your vocabulary.
• Exercise improves your health.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Finally, I gave up it.
column2:
✅ Finally, I gave it up.

📖 With separable phrasal verbs like 'give up', a pronoun object must go in the middle: 'give it up', not 'give up it'.

e.g.
• I gave it up last year.
• Please turn it off.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
In our traditional culture, there are many error in it.
column2:
✅ There are so many errors in our traditional culture.

📖 'Error' is countable, so use the plural 'errors' after 'many'. Also drop the extra 'in it' — 'in our traditional culture' already says where.

e.g.
• There are many errors in this report.
• She made a few mistakes.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The professor's article is so obscure that they can't understand of it.
column2:
✅ The professor's article is so obscure that they can't understand it.

📖 'Understand' is transitive — it takes a direct object with no preposition: 'understand it', not 'understand of it'.

e.g.
• I don't understand it.
• Can you understand this sentence?

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Many students think the professor's article is so obscure, so they don't want to select this project.
column2:
✅ Many students think the professor's article is so obscure, so they don't want to read it.

📖 Keep referring to the same thing: the object is 'the article', so use the pronoun 'it' in the second clause instead of switching to a new noun ('this project').

e.g.
• I bought a book, but I haven't read it yet.
• She wrote a report and sent it to her boss.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The work of a mechanic outweigh the tasks of an office worker, so his salary is higher than him.
column2:
✅ The work of a mechanic outweighs the tasks of an office worker, so his or her salary is higher than the latter.

📖 Two points: the singular subject 'the work' needs 'outweighs'; and a comparison must be parallel — compare salary to salary, so use 'than the latter' (the office worker's), not 'than him'.

e.g.
• His income outweighs his expenses.
• Her salary is higher than the national average.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
They waited for me for a long time, so I apologized for them.
column2:
✅ They waited for me for a long time, so I apologized to them.

📖 You apologize TO a person (and FOR the thing you did): 'apologize to them'. 'apologize for them' would mean apologizing on their behalf.

e.g.
• I apologized to my boss for being late.
• He should apologize to her.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Having an accident is a serious matter to me because it will break my routine life.
column2:
✅ Having an accident is a serious matter to me because it will break my daily routine.

📖 The natural noun phrase is 'daily routine', not 'routine life'. 'break/disrupt your daily routine' is the usual collocation.

e.g.
• Travelling disrupts my daily routine.
• Try to keep to your daily routine.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Today I were very busy.
column2:
✅ Today I was very busy.

📖 First-person singular 'I' takes 'was' in the past, not 'were'.

e.g.
• I was very busy yesterday.
• I was tired after work.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
She was fine, so we went back to home.
column2:
✅ She was fine, so we came back home.

📖 'Home' as a destination takes no preposition: 'go/come home', not 'to home'. When returning to where you are, 'come back' is more natural than 'go back'.

e.g.
• I came home late last night.
• Let's go home.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you overspeed on this street, you will be find by the traffic policeman.
column2:
✅ If you speed on this street, you will be fined by the traffic policeman.

📖 In the passive, use the past participle 'fined' (the verb 'fine' = to charge a penalty), not 'find': 'be fined by someone'.

e.g.
• He was fined for parking illegally.
• You will be fined if you litter.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
They gave me the wrong goods, so we needed to come back to get refund for it.
column2:
✅ They gave me the wrong goods, so we needed to come back to get the refund of it.

📖 Use the article and the right preposition: 'get the refund of it', not 'refund for it'. As a noun, 'refund' needs 'the'.

e.g.
• I asked for a refund of the ticket price.
• Can I get a refund on this?

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Recently only my wife and my daughter went to there.
column2:
✅ Recently only my wife and my daughter went there.

📖 'There' is already an adverb of place, so it takes no preposition: 'went there', not 'went to there'.

e.g.
• We went there last summer.
• I'll go there tomorrow.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
It is because we more familiar with it.
column2:
✅ It is because we are more familiar with it.

📖 Every clause needs a verb. With an adjective like 'familiar' you need the verb 'be': 'we are more familiar with it', not 'we more familiar'.

e.g.
• We are familiar with this area.
• They are more familiar with the rules than I am.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you feel boring or tired, you can't remember what you learned.
column2:
✅ If you feel bored or tired, you can't remember what you learned.

📖 (assistant note) Use the -ed adjective for how you feel: 'bored'. The '-ing' form ('boring') describes the thing that causes the feeling. 'You are bored' / 'the class is boring'.

e.g.
• I was bored during the meeting.
• The movie was boring.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
When I learning something, I want to make the process more interesting.
column2:
✅ Whenever I learn something, I want to make the process more interesting.

📖 Use the present simple 'I learn' after 'whenever/when' (not 'I learning'). 'Whenever' fits a repeated, habitual action.

e.g.
• Whenever I study, I turn off my phone.
• When I cook, I listen to music.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you can't see your monitor clearly, you need to more focus.
column2:
✅ If you can't see your monitor clearly, you need to focus more.

📖 The adverb 'more' goes after the verb it modifies: 'focus more', not 'more focus'.

e.g.
• You should practice more.
• I need to sleep more.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
Before 2021, every year they need to upgrade their smart glasses.
column2:
✅ Before 2021, every year they needed to upgrade their smart glasses.

📖 The time frame is past ('before 2021'), so use the past 'needed', not 'need'.

e.g.
• Back then, we needed to book tickets early.
• Last year they needed more staff.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
In my country, there are many social media that went bankrupt.
column2:
✅ In my country, there are many social media companies that went bankrupt.

📖 It's the companies, not 'social media' itself, that go bankrupt. Add the head noun: 'social media companies / platforms'.

e.g.
• Several tech companies went bankrupt that year.
• Many startups fail in their first year.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
A millionaire is someone who has a million money.
column2:
✅ A millionaire is someone who makes millions / has a million dollars.

📖 'Money' is uncountable, so 'a million money' is wrong. Use a unit ('a million dollars') or 'makes millions'.

e.g.
• She makes millions every year.
• He has a million dollars in the bank.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
For my grandfather, he was dead because they were poor.
column2:
✅ My grandfather died because they were poor.

📖 Use the verb 'died' for the event of dying. 'was dead' describes the state afterwards and sounds wrong here.

e.g.
• My grandfather died last year.
• He died at the age of 80.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
She had to stayed at bed every day.
column2:
✅ She had to stay in bed every day.

📖 After 'had to' use the base verb 'stay' (not 'stayed'). Also the set phrase is 'stay in bed' when resting or sick.

e.g.
• I had to stay home yesterday.
• He stayed in bed all weekend because he was sick.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you want to get education, you need to spend a lot of money to buy it.
column2:
✅ If you want to get an education, you need to pay a lot of tuition fees.

📖 Say 'get an education' (with 'an'), and you 'pay tuition fees' rather than 'buy' education.

e.g.
• She wants to get a good education.
• We have to pay the tuition fees by September.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If you are not at the top level, you can't easy find a suitable job.
column2:
✅ If you are not at the top level, you can't easily find a suitable job.

📖 Use the adverb 'easily' to modify the verb 'find', not the adjective 'easy'.

e.g.
• You can easily find it online.
• She easily passed the exam.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
It's too difficult find a suitable job in my country.
column2:
✅ It's too difficult to find a suitable job in my country.

📖 (assistant note) 'too + adjective' is followed by 'to + verb': 'too difficult to find'. You drop this 'to' often.

e.g.
• It's too hard to explain.
• The box was too heavy to lift.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
You are the owner of the company, so you need to consider for your employees.
column2:
✅ You are the owner of the company, so you need to consider your employees.

📖 'Consider' is transitive and takes a direct object with no preposition: 'consider your employees', not 'consider for'.

e.g.
• A good boss considers his staff.
• You should consider other people's feelings.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
If your company go bankrupt, all of your employees will lose their jobs.
column2:
✅ If your company goes bankrupt, all of your employees will lose their jobs.

📖 The singular subject 'your company' takes 'goes' in the present: 'the company goes bankrupt'.

e.g.
• If the deal falls through, the firm goes under.
• If he comes late, we start without him.

column1:
Fix this sentence:
The government can give them basic necessary even though they go bankrupt.
column2:
✅ The government can give them basic necessities even if they go bankrupt.

📖 Use the noun 'necessities' (the things you need), not the adjective 'necessary'. Use 'even if' for a hypothetical situation, not 'even though' (which states a fact).

e.g.
• The shelter provides basic necessities.
• I'll support you even if you fail.
